nodes.name: 0021_lumpy_supreme_intelligence.sql
nodes.summary: Drizzle migration adding a NOT NULL calendar_id foreign key to external_events, backfilling it from calendar_events/external_calendars, swapping the unique constraint to (provider, calendar_id, external_event_id), and resetting sync cursors for empty mirrors.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses a Postgres UPDATE ... FROM with a JOIN for the backfill; the UPDATE target (ee) is matched in WHERE rather than the FROM-list, per the inline comments.

nodes.name: 0022_long_demogoblin.sql
nodes.summary: Drizzle migration adding a NOT NULL jsonb calendar_order column (defaulting to an empty array) to user_settings for persisting calendar ordering.

nodes.name: 0023_time_date_format.sql
nodes.summary: Drizzle migration replacing user_settings.time_locale with explicit time_format ('24h') and date_format ('dmy') columns.

nodes.name: recurrence.ts
nodes.summary: RRULE recurrence engine that expands recurring events into occurrences within a range and provides helpers to split, join, exclude, and end recurrence series, backed by a parsed-rule cache.
nodes.languageNotes: Wraps the rrule library with a Map-based parse cache and iCal string manipulation for EXDATE/UNTIL editing.

nodes.name: caldav.ts
nodes.summary: Drizzle query functions for CRUD operations on CalDAV account records, storing only pre-encrypted credentials and supporting multiple accounts per user.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Drizzle onConflictDoUpdate for idempotent upserts keyed on (userID, serverUrl, username).

nodes.name: expandRecurringEvents
nodes.summary: Expands RRULE-bearing events into individual occurrences within a date range, keeping non-recurring events only when they overlap, with synthetic stable occurrence ids.

nodes.name: splitRecurrence
nodes.summary: Splits a stored recurrence string into the bare RRULE line and any extra lines such as EXDATE.

nodes.name: joinRecurrence
nodes.summary: Rebuilds a stored recurrence string from an RRULE and extra lines, adding explicit prefixes for multi-line output.

nodes.name: excludeOccurrence
nodes.summary: Excludes a single occurrence from a recurring series by appending an EXDATE line.

nodes.name: endSeriesBefore
nodes.summary: Ends a recurring series just before a given occurrence by setting an UNTIL clause, dropping any conflicting UNTIL/COUNT.

nodes.name: saveCaldavAccount
nodes.summary: Upserts a CalDAV account with an already-encrypted password, updating credentials on reconnection of the same server/user.

nodes.name: getCaldavAccountsByUser
nodes.summary: Returns the CalDAV account ids and connection metadata for a given user, used by the adapter's listAccounts.

nodes.name: getCaldavAccountById
nodes.summary: Fetches full credentials for one CalDAV account by id so the adapter can build a client, returning null when not found.

nodes.name: deleteCaldavAccount
nodes.summary: Deletes a CalDAV account scoped to both its id and the owning user.

nodes.summary: Drizzle queries for calendar invites: creating an invite (idempotent) and pruning expired ones.

nodes.name: sessions.ts

project.name: musubi
project.description: Open-source, self-hostable shared calendar. Events are freed from calendars and linked into many; two-way external sync with Google/Apple/CalDAV including recurring events with exceptions; shared calendars with roles; realtime. Expo SDK 56 client + Express API in a pnpm/turbo monorepo.

layers.name: Client UI
layers.description: Expo/React Native screens (app/) and reusable components including the custom Google-style calendar, rendering the mobile interface.

layers.name: Client Core & State
layers.description: Zustand stores, React contexts, hooks, API client, and client-side utilities that drive app state, data fetching, and offline behavior.

layers.name: API Server
layers.description: Express request handlers, middleware, permission checks, and transactional email templates exposing the REST/SSE backend.

layers.name: Sync Engine
layers.description: Provider-agnostic sync engine with Google and CalDAV adapters plus credential crypto, mirroring external calendars into Musubi.

layers.name: Shared Packages
layers.description: Cross-cutting workspace packages: Better Auth config, shared Zod types, recurrence/CalDAV calendar helpers, DB query modules, and shared config.

layers.name: Data & Persistence
layers.description: Drizzle ORM schemas, PostgreSQL and local SQLite migrations, and generated table definitions defining the calendar/event/membership data model.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: Astro-based docs site content, assets, and the project README for the self-hostable open-source project.

layers.name: Infrastructure & Config
layers.description: Dockerfiles, docker-compose stacks for self-hosting, and monorepo tooling (turbo, pnpm workspace, env, package manifests).

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to grasp what Musubi (結び, "the knot") is: an open-source, self-hostable shared calendar where events are freed from any single calendar and linked into many. It documents the core pillars this tour will unpack — two-way external sync (Google, Apple/iCloud, CalDAV), shared calendars with owner/editor/viewer roles, realtime updates, and a pnpm+turbo monorepo split into an Expo client and an Express API. Read this first to build the mental map before touching code.

tour.title: Monorepo Layout
tour.description: Before diving into either app, see how the workspace is wired together. turbo.json defines the dev/build task pipeline that orchestrates every workspace, while pnpm-workspace.yaml declares the apps/* and packages/* members that let the client and API share the db, types, auth, and calendar packages. Understanding this boundary explains why domain logic lives in packages/ and is imported by both runtimes.
tour.languageLesson: In a pnpm workspace, packages reference each other by name (e.g. @musubi/db) rather than relative paths, and Turborepo caches task outputs per-package so unchanged workspaces are skipped on rebuild.

tour.title: The Data Model
tour.description: Everything in Musubi hangs off the database schema. This Drizzle ORM schema defines every table: the auth tables (user, session, account), the calendar domain (calendars, events, calendarMembers, calendarEvents, eventUsers, calendarInvites), user profile tables, and the external-sync tables (externalCalendars, externalEvents, caldavAccounts). The many-to-many calendarEvents join is the concrete expression of the README's "events freed from calendars" idea — one event row can be linked into many calendars.
tour.languageLesson: Drizzle migrations are plain, ordered SQL files (0000_, 0001_, ...) generated from the TypeScript schema. Each migration is an incremental, forward-only change, so the numbered sequence is the authoritative history of how the data model evolved.

tour.title: Query Modules
tour.description: Sitting one layer above the schema, the query modules are the only way the rest of the backend touches the database. Building on the tables from Step 3, these files expose typed helpers for reading and writing calendars and events, keeping raw SQL out of the request handlers. This separation is why the API and sync engine can share identical, tested data access.

tour.title: Shared Types & Contracts
tour.description: The client and server never trust raw JSON — they speak through shared Zod schemas. The event type captures timing (with date coercion), multi-calendar membership, the origin calendar that governs edit rights, recurrence, and cancellation state. Because these live in a shared package, the same validation runs on both sides of the wire, and the inferred TypeScript types keep the Expo client and Express API in lockstep with the data model from Step 3.
tour.languageLesson: Zod schemas are runtime validators that double as the source of TypeScript types via z.infer. Defining the schema once gives you both request-time validation and compile-time types, eliminating the drift between validation logic and type declarations.

tour.title: Authentication & Recurrence
tour.description: Two cross-cutting shared packages round out the foundation. The Better Auth config wires the Postgres adapter, email+password with reset emails, Google social login with account linking, and a database hook that gives every new user a personal calendar. The recurrence engine expands RRULE-based recurring events into concrete occurrences and provides split/join/exclude/end helpers backed by a parsed-rule cache — the logic behind the README's "recurring events with exceptions."
tour.languageLesson: RRULE (RFC 5545) encodes a recurring event as a compact rule string like FREQ=WEEKLY;BYDAY=MO. Rather than storing every occurrence, the engine expands the rule on demand within a date window and caches the parsed rule, so an infinite series costs only the visible slice.

tour.title: API Server Entry Point
tour.description: With the shared foundation in place, meet the backend's front door. This Express entry point configures CORS, JSON parsing, and logging, mounts Better Auth (Step 6) under /api/auth, registers all /api/v1 routes with async error wrapping, and runs an hourly cleanup job for expired invites, sessions, and tombstones. It is the wiring diagram for every REST route the client calls.

tour.title: Event Handlers & Realtime
tour.description: Here is where the core business logic lives. The event handlers implement CRUD plus the signature link and fork operations across calendars, enforcing per-calendar permissions (Step 5), pushing changes to external providers through the sync engine, and notifying members over SSE. The stream module is that realtime backbone: it tracks connected clients and exposes notifyCalendarMembers, which handlers call to push live updates — the mechanism behind the README's "realtime" promise.
tour.languageLesson: Server-Sent Events (SSE) hold a one-way HTTP stream open from server to client. Unlike WebSockets they need no special protocol upgrade — the server keeps the response open and writes text/event-stream chunks, which is ideal for push-only notifications like calendar updates.

tour.title: The Sync Engine
tour.description: This is Musubi's most distinctive subsystem. The generic engine registers pluggable provider adapters, reconciles remote calendar lists with local mirrors, pulls incremental changes per-calendar via cursors, and pushes local event creates/updates/deletes back out. It depends only on the adapter contract, not on any specific provider — the design that lets Google, CalDAV, and future providers plug in without touching the engine, and the source of the handlers' two-way sync in Step 8.

tour.title: Provider Adapters & Credential Crypto
tour.description: Building on the adapter contract from Step 9, these are the concrete implementations. The Google adapter uses the Calendar v3 REST API with incremental sync tokens and translates recurrence between Google's format and Musubi's NormalizedEvent. The CalDAV adapter converts between iCalendar (via ICAL.js) and NormalizedEvent using etag-based change detection. Both rely on the crypto helper, which encrypts CalDAV app-passwords at rest with AES-256-GCM so third-party credentials are never stored in plaintext.
tour.languageLesson: AES-256-GCM is authenticated encryption: it both hides the data and detects tampering. A fresh random IV (initialization vector) per encryption is essential — reusing an IV with the same key catastrophically weakens GCM, so the code generates a new IV every time and stores it alongside the ciphertext.

tour.title: Client Bootstrap
tour.description: Now cross over to the Expo client. This root layout is the app's ignition sequence: it runs local SQLite migrations, loads fonts, checks the client version against the server, handles .ics deep links, applies the theme, and registers push notifications before rendering any screen. It is the client-side counterpart to the API entry point from Step 7 and the highest fan-out file on the client.
tour.languageLesson: Expo Router uses file-based routing: files under app/ become routes, and a _layout.tsx wraps everything beneath it. The root _layout is the natural place for one-time bootstrap (migrations, fonts, providers) because it mounts once and persists for the app's lifetime.

tour.title: The Custom Calendar UI
tour.description: This is the interface users actually see and touch. The TimelineView renders the day/week grid with a minutes-to-pixels layout driven by a shared, pinch-zoomable hour-height, layered gestures for drag-to-create and hold-to-move, and a live now-indicator, all paged via InfinitePager. The AddEventModal is the composer — a docked bottom sheet with date/time pickers, calendar pills, the recurrence-rule builder (surfacing Step 6's engine), and local notification scheduling.
tour.languageLesson: React Native Reanimated runs animations and gesture math on the UI thread via shared values, so pinch-zoom and drag stay at 60fps even while JS is busy. A single shared hourHeight value driving the layout is what lets the whole timeline rescale smoothly during a pinch.

tour.title: State, Offline & Deployment
tour.description: Finally, see how the client stays fast and works offline, then how the whole thing ships. Zustand stores hold events and calendars with optimistic local mutations that fall back to API-backed actions, keeping the expo-sqlite cache (services/db.ts) and notification schedules in sync on every change. The Dockerfile packages the API as a multi-stage image that runs migrations before boot, and the self-host Compose stack pairs it with an embedded Postgres 17 — the concrete realization of the README's "self-host via Docker" pitch.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use several FROM stages: a deps stage installs the pnpm workspace, a build stage compiles, and a lean runner stage copies only what is needed to boot. Running drizzle-kit migrations as part of container startup keeps the database schema in lockstep with the deployed image.
